name: Tierra de Campos Loop: El Burgo Ranero, Gordaliza, and Bercianos
name_original: Tierra de Campos – circular desde El Burgo Ranero por Gordaliza del Pino y Bercianos del Real Camino
description: This easy 20.8-mile touring cycling route from El Burgo Ranero explores the historical Tierra de Campos, passing through villages and open p
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around El Burgo Ranero traverse the flat, open plains of the Tierra de Campos region, characterized by extensive agricultural fields and a generally low elevation profile. The landscape offers long, straight sections ideal for touring cyclists seeking consistent terrain. Historical elements, such as ancient Roman bridges and traditional village squares, are integrated into many routes, providing cultural points of interest. Best touring cycling routes around El Burgo Ranero The most popular touring cycling route is Tierra de Campos Loop: El Burgo Ranero, Gordaliza, and Bercianos, a 20.7 miles (33 km) trail that takes 1 hour 54 minutes to complete. This easy route offers expansive views of the agricultural landscape. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Roman bridge – Plaza Mayor loop from El Burgo Ranero, a difficult 77.0 miles (124 km) path. This route features a mix of open plains and historical sites, including a Roman bridge and a Plaza Mayor. Local touring cyclists also love the El Burgo Ranero – Reliegos Meteorite Impact Site loop from Pozo Vega, a 22.8 miles (37 km) trail leading through open countryside to a unique geological feature, often completed in about 2 hours 10 minutes. Touring cycling around El Burgo Ranero is defined by open plains, agricultural fields, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy touring bike trails to more challenging long-distance routes. The routes in El Burgo Ranero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 150 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore El Burgo Ranero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around El Burgo Ranero?
A: There are 7 touring cycling routes around El Burgo Ranero, offering a variety of experiences across the flat plains of the Tierra de Campos. These include 4 easy routes, 2 moderate routes, and 1 difficult route.

Q: Are there any easy touring bike trails suitable for beginners or families in El Burgo Ranero?
A: Yes, El Burgo Ranero offers several easy touring bike trails perfect for beginners or families. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38155006" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albergue La Perala – Rest Area at Ermita de Perales loop from Estación ffcc El Burgo Ranero&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 30 km long and takes under two hours to complete. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31518516" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rest Area at Ermita de Perales – Bercianos del Real Camino loop from Calzadilla de los Hermanillos&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 23 km ride.

Q: What kind of historical or natural landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The routes often integrate historical elements and natural features. You can explore sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4441511" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Benito Arch and Ruins of the Monastery of San Benito&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5838156" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Our Lady of Perales&lt;/a&gt;. For a unique natural attraction, consider a route that passes the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6771999" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Reliegos Meteorite Impact Site&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular touring bike trails in the area?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes around El Burgo Ranero are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1031179474/tierra-de-campos-loop-el-burgo-ranero-gordaliza-and-bercianos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Campos Loop: El Burgo Ranero, Gordaliza, and Bercianos&lt;/a&gt; and the more challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38154968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman bridge – Plaza Mayor loop from El Burgo Ranero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in El Burgo Ranero?
A: The flat, open plains of El Burgo Ranero are generally pleasant for touring cycling during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summers can be hot and exposed, while winters may bring colder conditions. Always check the local weather forecast before heading out.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in El Burgo Ranero?
A: The komoot community rates the touring cycling routes in El Burgo Ranero highly, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the expansive views of the agricultural landscape, the quiet, consistent terrain, and the integration of historical sites into the routes.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: While specific cafes vary by route, the region features traditional villages where you can often find local establishments. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2926583" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bar Elvis&lt;/a&gt; in El Burgo Ranero is a local spot where you can take a break. Many routes also pass through small settlements like Gordaliza and Bercianos, offering opportunities for refreshments.

Q: What should I wear for touring biking in El Burgo Ranero?
A: Given the open plains, it's advisable to wear layers that can adapt to changing temperatures and protect you from the sun. Sunscreen, sunglasses, and a hat are recommended due to the lack of shade. Always bring sufficient water, especially on longer routes, as services can be spread out.

Q: Is it possible to find parking for touring bike trails in El Burgo Ranero?
A: El Burgo Ranero is a small town, and parking is generally available within the village, often near the main square or residential areas. For routes starting from specific points outside the main village, check the route details on komoot for any designated parking information.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots along the touring routes?
A: The primary scenic views in El Burgo Ranero come from the vast, open agricultural plains of the Tierra de Campos, offering expansive horizons and a sense of tranquility. Routes often pass through traditional villages and by historical landmarks, providing picturesque stops rather than elevated viewpoints.

Q: How long do the touring bike trails typically take to complete?
A: The duration of the touring bike trails varies significantly. Shorter, easy routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1031179474/tierra-de-campos-loop-el-burgo-ranero-gordaliza-and-bercianos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Campos Loop&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in under two hours (1 hour 54 minutes). Longer, more challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38154968" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman bridge – Plaza Mayor loop&lt;/a&gt;, can take over seven hours due to their 124 km distance.

Q: Can I find routes that connect El Burgo Ranero with other nearby towns or attractions?
A: Yes, many routes are designed to connect El Burgo Ranero with neighboring villages and points of interest within the Tierra de Campos region. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1031179474/tierra-de-campos-loop-el-burgo-ranero-gordaliza-and-bercianos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Campos Loop&lt;/a&gt; connects El Burgo Ranero with Gordaliza and Bercianos, allowing you to experience more of the local area.
tips: El Burgo Ranero is a town of about 700 inhabitants that has a shop, several accommodations for pilgrims and a taxi company in case you suffer any incident and need help to get to the end of your stage.

Small town of Mozarabic origin whose patron saint today is the Virgen del Carmen, to whom a hermitage is dedicated. The parish church is dedicated to Nuestra Señora de Arbás and is an interesting construction from the 12th century that consists of three naves and three apses with four floors decorated with arches.

The name of this town is due to the fact that its first settlers came from El Bierzo. It is a quiet place that remains relatively unchanged despite the large number of pilgrims who have passed through here for centuries. However, its lagoon has a disturbing legend about residents who drowned in it when they tried to escape the massacre of the warrior Gerinaldo.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is classified as an easy bike ride. It's designed to be accessible for various fitness levels, likely featuring mostly well-paved surfaces or compacted dirt tracks that are straightforward to cycle.

faqs.question: Where can I park when cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in El Burgo Ranero. You should be able to find parking within or near the village to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are there public transport options to reach the trailhead?
faqs.answer: While specific public transport details for El Burgo Ranero are not provided, the village is located in the province of León, Spain. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or train connections to nearby larger towns and then arrange onward travel to El Burgo Ranero.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the distinctive landscape of Tierra de Campos, known as the "Land of Fields." Expect vast, open countryside with extensive cultivated fields, often presenting golden hues. The route offers a quiet pace of life through small towns and glimpses of historical monuments.

faqs.question: What interesting places can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several notable locations. You'll start in El Burgo Ranero, cycle past the Church of Nuestra Señora de Arbás in Gordaliza del Pino, and visit Bercianos del Real Camino, a village on the historic Camino de Santiago, which also features a local lagoon.

faqs.question: What wildlife can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The Tierra de Campos region is ecologically significant, particularly for birdwatching. It boasts one of the highest concentrations of bustards in the Iberian Peninsula, and potentially the world. Keep an eye out for various bird species and other local wildlife in the open countryside.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the open plains and cultivated fields, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant cycling conditions with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot and exposed, while winters can be cold and potentially windy.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the open countryside of Tierra de Campos can be pleasant for dogs, specific regulations regarding dogs on this particular cycling route are not detailed. It's generally recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially when passing through villages or near cultivated fields, and to ensure they are well-behaved around other cyclists and livestock.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It is an open public path through the Tierra de Campos region.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Camino Francés - 04 Burgos a León, the Camino de Madrid - Melgar de Arriba -> Bercianos, and the broader Camino de Santiago. Other smaller paths like Travesía Norte 2 and Calle Norte 1 also cross the route.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay overnight near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through villages like El Burgo Ranero, Bercianos del Real Camino, and Gordaliza del Pino, you can expect to find local cafes, restaurants, and possibly small guesthouses or hostels, especially in Bercianos del Real Camino due to its location on the Camino de Santiago.
